0: <title>Comparative life cycle assessment of Flemish and Western Cape pork production </title>
L. Devers, T.E. Kleynhans & E. Mathijs

To cite this article: L. Devers, T.E. Kleynhans & E. Mathijs (2012) Comparative life cycle assessment of Flemish and Western Cape pork production, Agrekon, 51:4, 105-128, DOI: 10.1080/03031853.2012.741208

<title>COMPARATIVE LIFE CYCLE ASSESSMENT OF FLEMISH AND WESTERN CAPE PORK PRODUCTION </title>
L. Devers*, T.E. Kleynhans** and E. Mathijs***
1: <header>ABSTRACT </header>

This study compares a life cycle assessment (LCA) of pork production in the Western Cape with pork production in Flanders. The objectives of this study were to map and quantify the environmental impacts of producing pork in the Western Cape and exporting it to Antwerp in Flanders with the environmental impacts of producing pork in Flanders and delivering it to the same location. The impact categories included are (i) global warming potential (GWP), (ii) eutrophication potential, (iii) acidification potential, and (iv) energy use. By pointing out weak points in the different environmental impact categories, suggestions are made to lower the environmental burdens of pork production.

Four main activities of the pork production chain were covered by the LCA, namely: (i) the feed provision activity, which includes the production of raw materials and feed, (ii) the pig farming activity, (iii) the slaughter house activity and (iv) the slurry (treatment) activity. An additional (v) pork shipping activity was added in the case of the Western Cape pork chain. A cradle-to-gate life cycle assessment was carried out, with a functional unit (FU) of one kg of Western Cape or Flemish pork (carcass weight) delivered to the distribution centre in Antwerp.

Flemish GWP, eutrophication potential, acidification potential and energy use are 56%, 65%, 62% and 59% respectively of the Western Cape equivalents. The exporting of pork accounts for less than 8% of environmental impacts in all impact categories.

Potential exists in the Western Cape to compete on an environmental par when impacts are measured in terms of a per-area unit. It is therefore recommended that for future comparative South African-European LCA studies of pork or other intensive livestock or poultry production chains, an FU of one kg meat as well as an area unit be used when measuring total eutrophication and acidification impacts.
Keywords: Environmental impact; life cycle assessment; pork production; global warming potential; eutrophication potential; acidification potential

3: <header>1 INTRODUCTION </header>

Meat demand has increased significantly in recent decades and has grown faster than the global human population, due to (i) changing diets and (ii) a general increase in standards of living, allowing more people to afford meat (Keyzer et al., 2005). The forecast for global meat production towards 2050 is set at 465 million tonnes, which is more than double the production for 2000 (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Forecasts for 2015 are that on a global scale, pork will become the most important type of meat produced and consumed (FAPRI, 2006).

Traditionally, pigs were produced on small mixed farms in the Northern Hemisphere, together with other livestock and crops, which provided on-farm pig feed. Currently, pigs are produced on highly specialised farms, fed with concentrated (often imported) pig feed and are kept on concrete stalled floors. The resulting higher production and higher animal densities contribute to increased pollution of water, soil and air.

Historically, countries in the Northern Hemisphere have raised most of the livestock needed to fulfill their own needs. Pork is the most important agricultural product in Flanders, the northern region of Belgium. Some 95% of Belgian pork production takes place in Flanders (FAOStat, 2008; VCM, 2010). During recent decades some emerging countries, such as Brazil and Argentina, increased meat production and exports to Europe. They have a comparative advantage in agricultural production due to their favourable climatic conditions which allows them to be major producers of maize and soy beans.

Against this background, the South African Pork Producers' Organisation (SAPPO) showed an interest in increasing South African pork production for export to Europe. SAPPO describes the South African pork industry as "while small, a dynamic, well-organised agricultural industry that compares favourably with the rest of the world in terms of production outputs" (SAPPO, 2009).

The growing awareness of the impacts of intensive livestock production and transportation of feed and meat on the local and global environments, motivated this study to compare the environmental impacts of producing pork in the Western Cape and in Flanders for the Belgian market. Antwerp served as the common destination. Life cycle assessment (LCA) method was used to determine the environmental impacts of each of those supply chains. It was expected that the energy needed for heating pig houses in the Western Cape and transporting the pork to the Flemish export market, together with the associated emissions, would be lower than the energy needed and resultant emissions involved in producing pork in the colder Flemish climate. If this could be proven, SAPPO could use this information to start developing an image of South African pork in the export markets as environmentally friendlier produced than European pork. In order to take into account many different environmental impacts, the complete pig
production chain was covered by the LCAs done on the Western Cape and Flemish case studies.

6: Only a limited number of comparable LCA studies of pork production could be found in the literature:

|"Cederberg and Flysjö (2004) studied three possible ""future"" pig farming systems in Sweden. Various scenarios were used focusing on animal welfare, the environment and product quality at low prices. Through an LCA, the various environmental impacts of each system were studied. One of the main outcomes of the study was that improved pork production depends mainly on improved feed production." |A French study was performed on pig feeding patterns in Bretagne (Van der Werf et al., 2005). Fertilisation of crops, local VS non-local production of raw materials, and concentrations of copper and zinc in the feed were the main contributors to the environmental impacts of producing and delivering feed. The study recommends a switch from maize-based feeds towards wheat-based feeds. Since the results of this study were based on pig feed production and not on the total pork production chain, they are not included in Table 2. |"Basset-Mens and Van der Werf (2005) carried out another study in France, this time covering the entire pig production cycle. The LCA aimed at stressing the different environmental impacts of different scenarios, a ""scenario-based approach"". Contrary to Cederberg and Flysjö (2004), this study was undertaken on existing production systems. The scenarios compared were conventional ""good agricultural practice"" according to French production rules, a French organic scenario called ""organic agriculture"", and the ""red label"" scenario, which is a French quality label. In general, eutrophication and acidification were found to be the greatest impacts of pig production. The results were very dependent on the choice of functional unit (FU) ""impact per kg pork"" or ""per ha"". The uncertainty analysis in this study indicated that reliable estimations of emissions for NO 3' NH2 and NO, in the field were hard to accomplish." |The study by the Environmental Resources Management (ERM) commissioned by the Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board Meat Services (AHDBMS) compared the following pig farming methods in the UK, using LCA : (i) indoor versus outdoor breeding, (ii) slatted floors VS loose bedding and (iii) compound VS liquid feeding. Recommendations for achieving the biggest improvements were: (i) utilising feed as efficiently as possible, (ii) achieving higher numbers of pigs per litter, and (iii) managing slurry/manure in that reduce et al., 2009).

<list>Dalgaard (2007) focused on pig production in Denmark. His study mainly defends a more efficient use of nitrogen on pig farms and in pig feed production. In the overall production chain of pork, nitrous oxide (53%) and methane (10%) contributed to global warming, nitrate (63%) and ammonia (30%) to eutrophication potential, and ammonia (84%) to acidification potential. Heargues that GWP per kg of pork could be reduced by 5 percent if the digestibility improving enzyme xylanase were added to the pig feed. 
A study by the Silsoe Research Institute (Williams et al., 2006) commissioned by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) also conducted an LCA on pork production. The impacts shown by this study were somewhat higher than those recorded for other impact assessments. 
Carlsson-Kanyama (1998) compared the greenhouse gas emissions and energy consumption for different food products with one another. An LCA was undertaken for carrots, tomatoes, rice, dry peas and pork. The study shows that impacts were the highest for pork and the lowest for potatoes. Another finding was that the choice of FU had a decisive influence on the results of the study. The overall conclusion was that current food consumption (in developed countries) exceeded the level of sustainability by a factor of at least four. 
A country comparative study undertaken by Olea et al. (2009), Modelled standard and alternative pig production systems for two countries, namely Mexico and the UK. The UK scenarios were more self-sufficient in terms of feedstuffs, had lower pig manure fermentation rates, and had more integrated agricultural practices than did the Mexican scenarios. In both countries, methane and N2O were the main contributors to GWP, deposition of ammonia was the main source of acidification potential, and runoff of nitrate and phosphate accounted mainly for eutrophication potential. 
Other noteworthy LCA applications focused on animal feed production. For animal feed, extensive crop production is believed to be environmentally more favourable than intensive crop production (Eriksson, 2004). A German study concluded the same by pointing out that fertiliser rates below the economic optimum were preferable for all impact categories studied, except for land use (Brentrub et al., 2004). Eriksson (2004) also concluded that environmental impact categories sometimes show conflicting results. When optimising a system for acidification and eutrophication, the system may deteriorate in terms of GWP, which is why the results of the report are weighted by normalisation.</list>

10: <header>3 METHODOLOGY: LIFE CYCLE ASSESSMENT </header>

Life cycle assessment is a methodology that takes into account all the environmental effects of a product during its life cycle (Wegener Sleeswijk et al., 1996). The LCA methodology is described in international standards ISO 14040-14043 (ISO 14040, 1997; ISO 14041, 1998; ISO 14042, 1998; ISO 14043, 1998).
11: <header>3.1 History and general goal of LCA </header>

LCA emerged in the early 1970s. At that time, industrial companies used the method to assess their material flows, energy efficiency, raw materials consumption, and to some extent waste disposal. LCA has gained popularity since the UN Earth Summit in 1992. As LCA applications were often disappointing and led to false conclusions, ISO norms were set up to create some firm rules for its use (Jensen et al., 1997).

The general goal of a LCA is to study the environmental aspects and potential impacts throughout a product's or function's life (from cradle to grave) (ISO 14040, 1997). The entire life cycle of a product is included, namely the extraction of raw materials, manufacturing, distribution, transportation, maintenance, recycling, emissions and final disposal.

In addition to the cradle-to-grave approach, other approaches exist. Cradle-to- gate includes all processes from raw material extraction through to the production phase (gate of the factory), and is used to determine the environmental impact of producing goods. Gate-to-grave includes the processes from the usage of a product to its end-of-life phases (everything post production), and is used to determine the environmental impacts of a product once it leaves the factory. Gate- to-gate includes the processes starting with one production phase to the start of another, and is used to determine the environmental impacts of a single production step or process.

LCA provides a product-based indicator of impact on the environment, for example, 'global warming potential per kilogram of pork'. There are also area- based indicators, for example, 'nitrate leaching per hectare' (Halberg et al., 2005). Area-based indicators can be useful, for example, when a farm is situated in a nitrate vulnerable zone, but are useless when attempting to establish global warming potential, as this is not a local, but a global effect. Hence, product-based indicators like LCA are useful for evaluating the impact of food production on the global environment (Dalgaard et al., 2007). Other product-based indicators are "food miles" and "carbon footprint" (exclusively focused on global warming), but life cycle thinking remains the basic idea behind the product-based indicators. Life cycle thinking is one of the five key principles in the European Union's Integrated Product Policy (IPP) (European Commission, 2003) and is supported by the United Nations Environmental Programme (UNEP, 2004).
12: <header>3.1 History and general goal of LCA </header>

LCA emerged in the early 1970s. At that time, industrial companies used the method to assess their material flows, energy efficiency, raw materials consumption, and to some extent waste disposal. LCA has gained popularity since the UN Earth Summit in 1992. As LCA applications were often disappointing and led to false conclusions, ISO norms were set up to create some firm rules for its use (Jensen et al., 1997).

The general goal of a LCA is to study the environmental aspects and potential impacts throughout a product's or function's life (from cradle to grave) (ISO 14040, 1997). The entire life cycle of a product is included, namely the extraction of raw materials, manufacturing, distribution, transportation, maintenance, recycling, emissions and final disposal.

In addition to the cradle-to-grave approach, other approaches exist. Cradle-to- gate includes all processes from raw material extraction through to the production phase (gate of the factory), and is used to determine the environmental impact of producing goods. Gate-to-grave includes the processes from the usage of a product to its end-of-life phases (everything post production), and is used to determine the environmental impacts of a product once it leaves the factory. Gate- to-gate includes the processes starting with one production phase to the start of another, and is used to determine the environmental impacts of a single production step or process.

LCA provides a product-based indicator of impact on the environment, for example, 'global warming potential per kilogram of pork'. There are also area- based indicators, for example, 'nitrate leaching per hectare' (Halberg et al., 2005). Area-based indicators can be useful, for example, when a farm is situated in a nitrate vulnerable zone, but are useless when attempting to establish global warming potential, as this is not a local, but a global effect. Hence, product-based indicators like LCA are useful for evaluating the impact of food production on the global environment (Dalgaard et al., 2007). Other product-based indicators are "food miles" and "carbon footprint" (exclusively focused on global warming), but life cycle thinking remains the basic idea behind the product-based indicators. Life cycle thinking is one of the five key principles in the European Union's Integrated Product Policy (IPP) (European Commission, 2003) and is supported by the United Nations Environmental Programme (UNEP, 2004).

LCA consists of four interacting phases, namely (1) goal and scope definition, (2) life cycle inventory, (3) life cycle impact assessment and (4) interpretation of the results (see Figure 1).

15: The goal of the study was to identify the main environmental "hotspots", i.e. the processes with the highest environmental impacts in the pork chains of both Flanders and the Western Cape, and then to compare them with each other. Both the economic benefits (using LCC) and the environmental impacts (using LCA) of export between the two regions were evaluated, establishing its viability.
16: The following elements indicate the scope of the study:
17: The product, namely pork, is to be produced in the Western Cape and Flanders, and is to be consumed in Flanders.

The FU is the functional output of a product to which the assessed environmental impacts will be scaled (ISO 14040, 1997). The FU used in this study is defined as one kg of Western Cape or Flemish pork (carcass weight) delivered to a distribution centre in Antwerp. Carcass weight was chosen because the ratio of live weight to

carcass weight differs between the two regions. Different functional units give different impacts. For instance, using an FU of "one kg of bone- and fat-free meat" (as used by Cederberg and Flysjö, 2004) will give an overall higher impact than using an FU of "one kg of pig produced (live weight) at slaughter" as the environmental impacts of processing to deliver the former will be added. Carcass weight was chosen as it provides options of further processing like deboning or selling cuts in butcheries. The distribution centre in Antwerp was chosen in order to identify a common destination for pork exported from the Western Cape and produced in Flanders.

19: <header>3.2.2.3 Description of the two pork production chains </header>

In Flanders, official confidentiality restrictions excluded access to any particular pig farm, to serve as a case study. For Flanders, average data were used, which were available in different databases and from coordinating institutes. The average Flemish piggery was defined as a 223-sow unit. Such national or regional databases do not exist in South Africa. Aa specific 700-sow unit representing a fairly typical pig farm in the Western Cape was chosen as a case study. The Flemish and Western Cape examples are both closed piggeries where the piglets and weaners are raised as well as fattened.
20: System boundaries determine which parts of the production systems involved will be included or excluded from the LCA. Cut-off criteria are used to set the boundaries (cradle-to-grave, gate-to-grave, cradle-to-gate or gate-to-gate). The boundaries in this study were set, and the links or activities of the production chain are explained in Figure 2. The production chain concerned comprises three main activities:

<list>Inputs: This entails all the raw materials that flow into the pig farm. Feed provided to the pig farm is the main input. Before reaching the farm, some of the original raw materials will have travelled a long way, and many underlying processes are involved. Other inputs like water and energy impact the pig farm more directly. 
Closed pig farming activity: Piglets are raised and fattened on one farm. Specific feedstuffs for each stage of the pig's life are provided. The inputs include feed, energy, and water, among others. The outputs are slaughtered pigs, slurry and wastewater, among others. 
Meat processing and distribution: The live pigs are transported from the farm to the slaughterhouse, and processed. The processed pork is eventually transported to its national and international destinations.</list>

1: <header>3.2.2.5 Allocation of inputs and outputs to product and co-products </header>

Allocation is the partitioning and relating of inputs and outputs of a process to the relevant products and co-products. When performing a LCA for a product, co-products are also identified in the production chain. This means that all environmental effects cannot be attributed to one product, in this case one kg of pork (carcass weight). In this study, examples of co-products produced are soy oil, as a co-product of soybean meal, and pig intestines, which can be used to produce animal feed. The relevant ISO (ISO 14041, 1998) prescribes the allocation of environmental impacts to the product and co-products. The allocations to different products can be done according to various methods, such as allocation according
to mass, cost, or energy (ISO 14040, 1997). In this study, allocation according to mass was used, since most data requiring allocation were specified in terms of mass units.
2: <header>3.2.2.6 Impact categories </header>
The following environmental impact categories were included in this study (see Figure 3):

<list>Global warming potential (GWP): climate change This reflects an increase in temperature due to emissions of greenhouse gasses (GHG) like carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O). Sulphur hexafluoride, hydrocarbons and VOCs (volatile organic compounds) with minor impacts are also taken into account. The results are expressed in kg CO2 equivalents calculated over a time horizon of 100 years. The CML method was used in this study (see Wegener Sleeswijk et al., 1996). The CML 2001 is available in GaBi (see http://cml.leiden.edu/software/data-cmlia.html). 
Eutrophication potential (EP) The leaching, runoff, and volatilisation of increased concentrations of nitrates and phosphates into nearby ecosystems are generally the biggest contributors to eutrophication potential (Vezjak et al., 1998). The results are characterised in kg PO equivalents (CML method) (Wegener Sleeswijk et al., 1996). Other contributors are NO to air, NH3 to air and NO to water. Emissions of ammonia, nitrogen oxide and COD (chemical oxygen demand) are the main contributors to environmental impact. 
Acidification potential (AP) 
A sensitive (im-)balance between ecosystems' internal and external H+ sources, and internal H+ sinks of different capacities and reaction rates is the main cause of acidification (Lükewille and Alewell, 2008). The release of acidic gasses like SO, 2' NO. and NH3 has the most potential to react with water in the atmosphere and form acids like H2SO4 4 and HNO 3' The results are expressed in kg SO2 equivalents (CML method) (Wegener Sleeswijk et al., 1996). Ammonia, sulphur dioxide, nitrogen oxides and hydrogen fluoride are the most important contributing emissions in this study. 
Energy use The total energy used in the pork chain is a measure of the efficiency in using renewable and non-renewable power. The results are expressed in MJ equivalents (Wegener Sleeswijk et al., 1996).</list>

5: <header>3.2.2.7 Assessment method </header>

The computer program used in this study for calculating impacts was the GaBi 4 program. This software is maintained by PE International. The program translates inputs and outputs into environmental impacts in different impact categories. For this study, the incorporated "GaBi 2006" database was used. It is the most comprehensive, up-to-date life cycle inventory database available (GaBi, 2009). The applicability of the data for South African conditions was monitored.
6:
<list>The construction of buildings and machinery were excluded from the study. Van Zeijts and Reus (1996) concluded that the contribution of buildings to overall impacts is minor, only 0-2%. 
The production and use of medicines were not included in the study, as quantities used are negligible and therefore account for a minimal proportion of environmental harm (see Cederberg and Flysjö (2004); Olea et al. (2009)). 
Disinfectants, detergents, washing products and other minor materials were excluded (Cederberg and Flysjö, 2004). 
Pesticides were also excluded due to a lack of data. LCA specialists argue that the use of pesticides is less important than the use of fertilizer and is more or less the same (not the brands but the active compounds) for the two regions.</list>
7: The second phase of an LCA involves developing a life cycle inventory (LCI) (see Figure 1), which entails collecting data and compiling and quantifying inputs and outputs in all the involved processes. The product chain for pork consists of three main activities: (i) feed production activity, (ii) pig farming activity and (iii) slaughterhouse activity. An additional activity (iv) manure treatment was incorporated. In the case of the feed production activity, many sub-activities occur. For the economic analysis, the exchange rate from rands to euros was fixed at ZAR9=1 euro (www.x-rates.com).
8: The life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) uses life cycle inventory data for all relevant emissions. First, all emissions must be classification in terms of the relevant impact categories. Then they must be characterised by one significant equivalent. All emissions that contribute to global warming are converted to kg CO2-equivalents according to the relevant characterisation factor. Each emission has its own characterisation factor (see Figure 3).
9: In this phase, the data of the LCI and LCIA are analysed and conclusions are drawn. The "significant issues" that contribute most to the results for the product must be emphasised. The results must be evaluated by implementing completeness, sensitivity and consistency checks to enhance the reliability of the study. In short, the LCI is intended to identify, qualify, check, evaluate and present conclusions SO that they are consistent with the goals and scope of the study (ISO 14043, 1998).
10: <header>4.1 Global warming potential </header>

This study shows total GWP values of 2.55 kg CO2-eq (FU)-1 for the Flemish pork chain and 4.5 kg CO2-eq (FU)-1 for the Western Cape chain (see Figure 4). The Flemish GWP value is of the same magnitude as former European LCA studies on pork, except for Williams et al. (2006), who show higher values for all emissions, due to (i) broader system boundaries linked to a more inclusive FU, (ii) higher ammonia emissions per kg of pork, (iii) a different method used for calculating nitrous oxides and (iv) higher nitrate leaching during soya been growth (see also Kingston et al., 2009).
The absolute difference in GWP between the Western Cape and Flanders is greatest for the feed provision activity (0.68 kg CO2-eq (FU)-1), second for the

slaughterhouse activity (0.53 kg CO2-eq (FU)-1), third the pig farming activity (0.34 CO2-eq (FU)-1), fourth for the pig slurry treatment activity (0.23 kg CO2-eq (FU)-1) and last for the pork shipping activity (0.16 kg CO2-eq (FU)-1). Pork shipping, which in this study only occurs from the Western Cape to Flanders, accounts for a minor GWP share of 3.7% of total Western Cape GWP.

Looking at the relative GWP contribution of each activity to the total GWP of the pork chain, the shipping activity shows the greatest difference between the Western Cape and Flanders (as no shipping component was included in the Flemish chain), and is taken as 100%; second is the slaughterhouse activity (94%); third is the pig farming activity (79%); fourth is the feed provision activity (40%); and the smallest relative difference is to be found in the pig slurry treatment activity (14%).

The main cause of the higher GWP of the slaughterhouse activity in the Western Cape activity lies in its higher electricity use, 1.66 MJ (FU) for Western Cape compared with 0.28 MJ (FU) for Flanders. This, added to the higher impact of electricity per MJ in the Western Cape due to the use of coal, gives a GWP ten times higher for the Western Cape than for Flanders. In addition, gas combustion plays a role in the higher GWP of the Western Cape slaughterhouse. Electricity is also the key GWP determinant for the pig farming activity. Electricity use at pig farms in the Western Cape is almost double that used in Flanders, the main reason for this being that in the Western Cape heating for weaners is provided by heat bulbs. Central heating, and more specifically, floor heating is used in Flanders.

The GWP contribution of providing concentrated pig feed in the Western Cape pork chain is 38 percent, and in Flanders it is 41 percent. These values are slightly lower than the findings of Blonk et al. (1997) and Carlsson-Kanyama (1998), namely that the GWP of concentrated pig feed production accounts for 47 and 53 percent in the respective pork chains.

The emissions caused by the slurry and slurry treatment activities are more or less the same for the Western Cape and Flanders. Factors influencing the GWP of this activity are (i) the feed conversion ratio (2.97 for Flanders and 3.45 for the Western Cape), (ii) the live weight at slaughter (112.5 kg for Flanders and 90- 100 kg for the Western Cape), (iii) the difference in feed diets and (iv) the slurry treatment. The added impact of these factors results in the Western Cape producing 14.28 kg of slurry (FU)-1 compared with 14.01 kg slurry (FU)-1 in Flanders.

Figure 5 gives the breakdown of GWP impacts for feed raw materials. Soybean meal is imported from overseas by feed factories in both the Western Cape and Flanders. In both cases, soybean has the highest GWP impact, which can be attributed to (i) the importation distance and (ii) production of the soybean meal and allocation of the soybean oil co-product. The difference in impact of soybean meal on GWP between the two regions is because in the Western Cape, 15 percent of pig feed consists of soybean meal and in Flanders this figure is only 7 percent.

The main cause of the higher GWP of the slaughterhouse activity in the Western Cape activity lies in its higher electricity use, 1.66 MJ (FU) for Western Cape compared with 0.28 MJ (FU) for Flanders. This, added to the higher impact of electricity per MJ in the Western Cape due to the use of coal, gives a GWP ten times higher for the Western Cape than for Flanders. In addition, gas combustion plays a role in the higher GWP of the Western Cape slaughterhouse. Electricity is also the key GWP determinant for the pig farming activity. Electricity use at pig farms in the Western Cape is almost double that used in Flanders, the main reason for this being that in the Western Cape heating for weaners is provided by heat bulbs. Central heating, and more specifically, floor heating is used in Flanders.

The GWP contribution of providing concentrated pig feed in the Western Cape pork chain is 38 percent, and in Flanders it is 41 percent. These values are slightly lower than the findings of Blonk et al. (1997) and Carlsson-Kanyama (1998), namely that the GWP of concentrated pig feed production accounts for 47 and 53 percent in the respective pork chains.

The emissions caused by the slurry and slurry treatment activities are more or less the same for the Western Cape and Flanders. Factors influencing the GWP of this activity are (i) the feed conversion ratio (2.97 for Flanders and 3.45 for the Western Cape), (ii) the live weight at slaughter (112.5 kg for Flanders and 90- 100 kg for the Western Cape), (iii) the difference in feed diets and (iv) the slurry treatment. The added impact of these factors results in the Western Cape producing 14.28 kg of slurry (FU)-1 compared with 14.01 kg slurry (FU)-1 in Flanders.

Figure 5 gives the breakdown of GWP impacts for feed raw materials. Soybean meal is imported from overseas by feed factories in both the Western Cape and Flanders. In both cases, soybean has the highest GWP impact, which can be attributed to (i) the importation distance and (ii) production of the soybean meal and allocation of the soybean oil co-product. The difference in impact of soybean meal on GWP between the two regions is because in the Western Cape, 15 percent of pig feed consists of soybean meal and in Flanders this figure is only 7 percent.

2: <header>4.2 Eutrophication potential </header>

Figure 6 shows the contribution of the various activities to eutrophication impact. The total eutrophication potential of the Flemish pork production chain is 0.022 kg PO4-eq (FU)-1 , and for the Western Cape it is 0.034 kg PO4-eq (FU)-1, , that is, 50% more than for Flanders. Flemish eutrophication potential is similar to those of other European LCA studies on pork (see Table 2).

In the Western Cape, the main causes of eutrophication are liquid slurry handling (85%) and feed production (10%). In Flanders, liquid slurry handling contributes 58%, and solid slurry handling 25% of total eutrophication impacts. The main contributor to eutrophication is leaching of N from pig manure and, to a lesser extent, ammonia emissions to the atmosphere and phosphate leaching. The eutrophication impacts of the slaughterhouse, pig farming and pork shipping activities are negligible.

In the Western Cape, the eutrophication impact of pig slurry treatment is 0.0078 kg PO4-eq (FU)- more than in Flanders, and for the feed provision activity, it is 0.0023 kg PO4-eq (FU)- more. Regarding relative differences between the two regions, the feed provision activity (64%) ranks highest, followed by the pig slurry treatment activity (27%). Diet and the pig slurry treatment methods have a larger influence on eutrophication potential than on GWP. Dalgaard et al. (2007) argue that digestibility of the feed has large impacts on eutrophication potential.

The choice of functional unit - one kg of pork (carcass weight) - in this study favours the Flemish pork chain. If the eutrophication potential were measured in terms of an area unit, the outcome would have been more favourable for the Western Cape, as this region is in a far better position to accommodate pig slurry than Flanders. In Flanders, removal of excess manure are controlled by government. The manure needs to be transported, often over large distances to less vulnerable areas, while in the Western Cape, there is a constant demand for manure, and the solid part can be spread locally.

The potential eutrophication impact of the feed provision activity for the Western Cape is more than double that of Flanders. This is caused mainly by the maize component of Western Cape pig feed, which contributes almost half of the eutrophication potential.
Pork shipping from the Western Cape to Flanders contributes only 1.4% of eutrophication potential.

6: In the Western Cape, the causes of acidification are liquid slurry handling (57%), pig feed production (16%), electricity use of the slaughterhouse (9%) and pig farming (7%). In Flanders, the main acidification contributors are liquid slurry handling (75%) and pig feed production (20%). Figure 8 gives the contribution of the various pork chain activities to the acidification impact category.

The total acidification impact of the Flanders pork chain is two thirds that of the Western Cape pork chain. In the Western Cape, half of this impact is caused by treating pig slurry; in Flanders, 80 percent of the acidification impact is caused by this activity. The main contributor to acidification in treating pig slurry is ammonia emissions to the air. The pig farming and slaughterhouse activities contribute negligibly in this regard in Flanders, but each account for 15 percent in the Western Cape. Eight percent of total acidification potential for the Western Cape chain is due to pork shipping.

In absolute terms, the Flemish and Western Cape pork production chains generate 0.039 kg SO2-eq (FU)-1 and 0.063 kg SO2-eq (FU)-1 respectively. The Flemish acidification impact corresponds with other European studies.

The absolute difference in total acidification potential between the Western Cape and Flanders is greatest for the pig slurry treatment activity (0.0067 kg SO2- eq (FU)-1), followed by the slaughterhouse activity, pork shipping activity, pig farming activity and feed provision activity (0.0057, 0.0052, 0.0043 and 0.0024 kg SO2-eq (FU)- respectively). For the slaughterhouse and pig farming activities, the differences in acidification potential between the Western Cape and Flanders can be explained in terms of the much higher electricity use of the former region. The relative differences in acidification potential for the feed provision activity are
not that large, because the maize and wheat production acidification impacts do not differ that much.

The total acidification impact of the Flanders pork chain is two thirds that of the Western Cape pork chain. In the Western Cape, half of this impact is caused by treating pig slurry; in Flanders, 80 percent of the acidification impact is caused by this activity. The main contributor to acidification in treating pig slurry is ammonia emissions to the air. The pig farming and slaughterhouse activities contribute negligibly in this regard in Flanders, but each account for 15 percent in the Western Cape. Eight percent of total acidification potential for the Western Cape chain is due to pork shipping.

In absolute terms, the Flemish and Western Cape pork production chains generate 0.039 kg SO2-eq (FU)-1 and 0.063 kg SO2-eq (FU)-1 respectively. The Flemish acidification impact corresponds with other European studies.

The absolute difference in total acidification potential between the Western Cape and Flanders is greatest for the pig slurry treatment activity (0.0067 kg SO2- eq (FU)-1), followed by the slaughterhouse activity, pork shipping activity, pig farming activity and feed provision activity (0.0057, 0.0052, 0.0043 and 0.0024 kg SO2-eq (FU)- respectively). For the slaughterhouse and pig farming activities, the differences in acidification potential between the Western Cape and Flanders can be explained in terms of the much higher electricity use of the former region. The relative differences in acidification potential for the feed provision activity are
not that large, because the maize and wheat production acidification impacts do not differ that much.

10: <header>4.4 Energy use </header>

In this study, only energy inputs, and not energy outputs, have been taken into account. Energy outputs are of several types, for example, waste heat that can be reused in the pork chain, non-utilised primary energy - such as wind and solar power - and energy generated through anaerobic digestion.

Figure 10 shows the total energy use of the pork chains, resulting from the use of natural gas, kerosene, diesel and electricity. Potential or existing outflows of energy are not included. The feed provision activity in both regions uses the most energy. Pork shipping from the Western Cape to Flanders accounts for seven percent of total energy used.

Total energy used in the pork production chain is 18.3 MJ (FU)-1 in Flanders and 30.7 MJ (FU)-1 in the Western Cape. The energy used by the Flemish pork chain is similar to the findings of other European studies. The absolute difference in total energy use between the Western Cape and Flanders is greatest for the slaughterhouse activity (6.07 MJ (FU)-1), second for the feed provision activity (2.5 MJ (FU)-1), third for the pork shipping activity (2.1 MJ (FU)-1), and least the pig farming activity (1.72 MJ (FU)-1). The energy use of the slurry treatment activity is included in the energy use of the pig farming activity, since the energy for slurry separation comes from the pig farm; also, trucks exporting the slurry are incorporated in the pig farming activity.

The relative differences within each activity are as follows (ranked in order of greatest to least impact): pork shipping activity (100% difference); slaughterhouse activity (82%), pig farming activity (37%) and feed provision activity (16%).

Figure 10: Energy use for pork chain activities in Western Cape and Flanders (MJ per functional unit)

The significant differences in impacts of electricity use between the two regions are due to the higher electricity use per functional unit in the Western Cape, and the different sources of power in the regions.

The higher sulphur content of South African diesel causes a higher acidification potential (Fritsche and Schmidt, 2006). In European countries, the maximum allowed sulphur level of diesel is 10 ppm; the common level of sulphur content in developing countries is 1000 ppm (Reinhardt et al., 2007). For detailed compositions of the South African and European diesel mixes, see the GaBi 2006 database.

In Flanders, electricity is generated mainly by nuclear power, while in South Africa it is generated mainly by coal combustion (Consult the GaBi 2006 database for details). The differences in environmental impacts of using one unit of diesel and electricity in the Western Cape and Flanders are shown in Table 1.

16: Identifying and quantifying the main environmental impacts of the pork life cycle chain revealed the contributions of the various pork production activities to the various impact categories. The feed provision activity, which includes the cultivation of land and production of raw materials, has been shown to have the greatest impact on energy use and GWP, while manure (treatment) has the greatest impact on eutrophication and acidification potential.

The greatest absolute difference in impact between the Western Cape and Flanders is in the feed provision activity. In the Western Cape, maize comprises more than 60 percent of pig feed, while in Flanders, it comprises only 7 percent, with wheat and wheat co-products making up 50 percent of Flemish pig feed. A shift in the main feed ingredient can reduce impacts for all categories. Also, soybean meal in the feed contributes significantly to total impacts. By reducing the soybean meal content in Western Cape feed by half, a GWP reduction of 7.5 percent

would be possible. A final factor influencing the impact categories for Western Cape pig feed production is the large distance maize has to travel before reaching the feed factory. Maize travels around 1200 km before reaching the factory in the Western Cape. Road transport contributes 3.3 percent of total GWP, half a percent of eutrophication potential and 1.4 percent of acidification potential.

The slaughterhouse and pig farming activities show the greatest relative differences, due to differences in diesel composition and energy generation between the Western Cape and Flanders. For the pig farming activity, improvements can be made by limiting energy use by more efficient heating.

The feed conversion factor for pigs in Flanders is 2.97, and for pigs in the Western Cape, it is 3.45. This significant difference is caused by diet and genetics, which have a direct impact on manure excretions. These interactions need further research. Attention needs to be paid to new techniques like anaerobic digestion, since they show promising results for improving the impacts of generating and treating pig slurry in South Africa.

In all impact categories, the Western Cape scores lower than Flanders when an FU of one kg of pork meat is used as the basis of comparison, signalling environmentally less friendly pork production in the Western Cape. Furthermore, one kg of pork is more expensive in the Western Cape than in Flanders. However, a too-narrow perspective on these results could be misleading, since in this study an FU of one kg of pork meat was chosen, with no spatial factors taken into account. Total eutrophication and acidification impacts are more harmful in Flanders than in the Western Cape, because of intensive farming on a small area. Potential exists in the Western Cape to compete on an environmental par when impacts are measured in terms of a per-area unit. This is the case because overseas transport accounts for less than eight percent of impacts in all categories. It is therefore recommended that for future comparative South African-European LCA studies of pork or other intensive livestock or poultry production chains, an FU of one kg meat as well as an area unit be used when measuring total eutrophication and acidification impacts.

Potential exists in the Western Cape to compete on an environmental par when impacts are measured in terms of a per-area unit. This is the case because overseas transport accounts for less than eight percent of impacts in all categories. 19: Identifying and quantifying the main environmental impacts of the pork life cycle chain revealed the contributions of the various pork production activities to the various impact categories. The feed provision activity, which includes the cultivation of land and production of raw materials, has been shown to have the greatest impact on energy use and GWP, while manure (treatment) has the greatest impact on eutrophication and acidification potential.

The greatest absolute difference in impact between the Western Cape and Flanders is in the feed provision activity. In the Western Cape, maize comprises more than 60 percent of pig feed, while in Flanders, it comprises only 7 percent, with wheat and wheat co-products making up 50 percent of Flemish pig feed. A shift in the main feed ingredient can reduce impacts for all categories. Also, soybean meal in the feed contributes significantly to total impacts.
